content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


April 14, 2004



Barry L. Matthews, Treasurer
Friends of Barbara Matthews 2004
Post Office Box 211
Tracy, CA 95378

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-075

Dear Mr. Matthews:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of "Friends of Barbara Matthews 2004" for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

To which election, the primary or general, should a replacement check to your committee be attributed if the contribution was originally intended for and received prior to the March 2004 primary election, but was subsequently found to be unsigned, returned by the bank, and lost by the contributor?

CONCLUSION

Under the facts specified below, the replacement check may be attributed to the primary election.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-075
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the treasurer of the "Friends of Barbara Matthews 2004," an Assembly
candidate committee. Your committee received a check during the late contribution
reporting period for the March 2, 2004, primary election on February 17, 2004. You filed
a late contribution report for the contribution received. After depositing the check, it was
returned to you for lack of a signature. You subsequently returned the check to the
contributor requesting that it be signed and returned to you. The contributor notified you
that the original check had been lost, but that the contributor was willing to write a new
check for the same amount as the original. You have a copy of the unsigned check in
your committee's records, in addition to the bank statement indicating that the check was
rejected for lack of a signature. In our phone conversation of April 2, 2004, you stated
that the committee does not have net debt from the primary election.

ANALYSIS

Section 85316 restricts a candidate for state elective office from accepting a
contribution after the date of an election.

"A contribution for an election may be accepted by a
candidate for elective state office after the date of the
election only to the extent that the contribution does not
exceed net debts outstanding from the election, and the
contribution does not otherwise exceed the applicable
contribution limit for that election."

Although the committee has no net debt outstanding from the primary election
the contribution in question was intended for and received prior to the primary election.
(Section 84211; regulation 18421.1.) Since you reported the receipt of the contribution
and have documentation of its attempted deposit, we conclude the contributor may write
a replacement check for the same amount as the original and the committee may allocate
the contribution to the primary election.

Regulation 18421.4 requires a committee controlled by a candidate for elective
state office to disclose the cumulative total(s) of contributions received or made for each
election. In order to fulfill this requirement, the Commission established instructions in
its Campaign Manual 1—Information for State Candidates, their Controlled
Committees, and Primarily Formed Committees for State Candidates, on page 7-10, for
instructions for the "Per Election" column state that "For each itemized contribution,
disclose the type of election, the year of the election, and the amount received from the
contributor for that election." Thus, an appropriate notation for the contribution,
assuming a $1,000 contribution, and that no other contributions had been received from
this contributor for this election, would be "$1,000 P-04."

File No. A-04-075
Page No. 3

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant II
